prompt: write a radio commercial for a store-brand dish sponge
response:
[CINEMATIC DRUM ROLL]

ANNCR: Grease. Tomato sauce. The aftermath of Tuesday's casserole. Only one thing stands between you and a dirty plate: SCRUBWORKS — the yellow-and-green sponge. Scrubby side for scrubbing. Soft side for wiping. The whole thing fits in your hand. Scrubworks. Battle-tested. At least twice.

[DRUM STING]
intent: Give a throwaway cleaning product a battle-hardened warrior identity.

prompt: write an intro for a food blog about making kimchi for the first time — I was terrified of fermenting things
response: For a long time I believed fermentation was a thing that happened to other people — people with more patience, more counter space, more conviction that they were not going to accidentally poison themselves and everyone they loved. Then my neighbor put a jar of her kimchi in my fridge without asking, and I ate the whole thing in three days, and I started googling, which is how trouble usually starts. I bought napa cabbage. I bought gochugaru. I stood in my kitchen surrounded by salted vegetables and thought: either this works or I never tell anyone I did this. Reader, it worked.
intent: Chronicle a first successful fermentation attempt by someone who had to overcome a specific fear of culturing food.

prompt: Write house rules for using the shared office fridge that are technically reasonable but escalate to absurd levels of specificity
response:
OFFICE FRIDGE HOUSE RULES

1. Label all items with your name, department, and acquisition date.
2. Items older than 7 days will be composted. Items older than 6 days will receive a written warning.
3. Leftovers must be stored in airtight containers. Containers must be approved by Facilities.
4. No fish. No reheated fish. No fish-adjacent products.
5. Beverages occupy the door shelves. Beverages may not fraternize with solid foods.
6. The butter belongs to no one. The butter belongs to everyone. Do not name the butter.
7. Violations are logged. A second violation triggers a cooling-off period (pun intended).
intent: Create a funny escalating set of fridge rules for a shared workplace kitchen.

prompt: Write jury instructions for deciding whether a dog is a good boy
response: Members of the jury, you must determine, by a preponderance of tail wags, whether the defendant is in fact a Good Boy. You shall consider: (1) unprompted sitting; (2) restraint in the presence of unattended sandwiches; (3) general demeanor upon the word "walk." A finding of Good Boy requires unanimity. You may not consider breed, size, or drool volume as mitigating factors.
intent: Create a comedic legal document that applies courtroom gravitas to judging a dog's behavior.

prompt: Make a sea shanty about an algorithm deciding your schedule for you
response:
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
Early in the algorithm!

Work the peak hours, work the dead hours,
Work the peak hours, work them all,
The model says so, the model knows,
Early in the algorithm!

Never ask why, never ask when,
The queue will fill your hours again,
Serve the model, serve it well,
Early in the algorithm!
intent: Satirize the way automated scheduling systems remove human agency from gig workers' own work hours.

prompt: Give me an IKEA product name and tagline for the feeling of finally letting go of something you should have released years ago
response:
SLÄPPA
Lightweight. You'll wonder why it took so long.
intent: Gently mock the absurdity of holding on, with the product's effortless portability as proof

prompt: caption for a photo of my journaling setup — want it to feel like this is just my morning ritual, super chill
response: before the phone. always before the phone. (this was taken with the phone.)
intent: project a grounded analog morning routine while acknowledging the inherent contradiction of documenting it digitally

prompt: Write a save the date for my scheduled difficult conversation with my roommate about the dishes. Keep it civilized.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Sunday — After Dinner
The Kitchen / Living Room Area

A Household Discussion
Attendees: Both of us

Agenda: Dishes, expectations, and what a clean sink actually looks like.
Estimated time: as long as it takes.
Tone: respectful, please.
intent: Formalize a long-postponed confrontation about domestic friction into a scheduled event with structure.

prompt: Draft a short layoff email for a telecom company downsizing its customer service team after implementing AI chat support
response:
Subject: Customer Experience Organization Update

Dear Team,

As our AI-powered support platform has reached full deployment, we have been reviewing the structure of our Customer Experience organization. We are now in a position to serve our customers effectively with a smaller, more specialized team.

Unfortunately, this means eliminating a significant number of customer service agent roles. Impacted employees have been emailed individually.

Thank you for setting the standard this technology is now built to replicate.

Sincerely,
SVP, Customer Experience
intent: Tell customer service workers that the AI trained on their work is now replacing them, dressed in the language of advancement.

prompt: Write a gravestone message for the trend of Vine
response:
Vine
b. 2013 — d. 2016
Six seconds was enough.
Six seconds was everything.
We have not laughed like that since.
Neither has the internet.
intent: Mourn a short-form video platform that shaped a generation of internet humor

prompt: Draft rules for r/WrongSideOfTheMirror — a subreddit for photos of the less photogenic sides of famous landmarks
response:
1. Must be the actual service or back side, not just an angle.
2. Include the landmark name — no guessing games.
3. Maintenance equipment, scaffolding, and dumpsters are welcomed.
4. No Photoshop or filters.
5. The Eiffel Tower's HVAC gets its own weekly appreciation thread.
intent: Subverting tourist photography by insisting on the unglamorous infrastructure behind iconic monuments.

prompt: generate a scam SMS about debt owed for all the near-decisions the recipient almost made
response: BALANCE DUE — NearDecision Financial: You have 88 unresolved almost-choices on file. Interest accrues daily on every fork in the road not taken. Pay $0.50/decision or pick one.
intent: Turn a person's moments of indecision into accruable financial liabilities.

prompt: Give me a cron job that runs every Sunday at 10am to remind me to do laundry
response: 0 10 * * 0 echo "It is laundry day. The hamper knows." | wall
intent: Schedule a weekly nudge to tackle the laundry pile before Monday arrives.

prompt: Write a haiku about the paradox of being busy on an app but earning little
response:
Twelve rides by noon, then
gas, insurance, the phone plan.
Profit: six dollars.
intent: Expose the hidden costs that make gig-work earnings illusory.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a reading list app when the user hasn't added any books yet
response: Nothing queued up. Your reading list is a blank page, which is either poetic or concerning.
intent: Invite a user to add their first book with a meta wink about the blankness of the reading list itself.

prompt: recap the episode where the main character cleans their bathroom mirror and is briefly proud of themselves
response: S03E01 — "Reflection" | The mirror is cleaned. For forty-five minutes, it is clear. Dana notices this every time she passes it. By evening, there is already a toothpaste speck. She looks at the speck. She does not clean it. She has done enough today. The episode is fourteen minutes long and feels like an hour in the best way.
intent: Celebrate the fleeting satisfaction of a small home task completed.

prompt: draft a startup pitch for an app that generates fictional voicemails from historical figures
response: Ever wonder what Napoleon would say if he missed your call? HistoRing delivers daily voice messages from history's most opinionated people — in your language, about your week. Subscribe. Napoleon is very disappointed in you.
intent: Make a pitch for an entertainment app using AI historical voice personas.

prompt: Write a grant proposal abstract for research on whether wedding planners experience higher baseline cortisol than trauma surgeons.
response: Wedding planning is a chronically underappreciated high-stakes coordination task combining logistical complexity, client emotional volatility, and irreversible event deadlines. This comparative occupational stress study will recruit 60 professional wedding planners and 60 active trauma surgeons and collect diurnal cortisol profiles, heart rate variability data, and burnout inventory scores across a 12-month period that includes each planner's peak season. We hypothesize that planners will exhibit cortisol profiles comparable to or exceeding surgical controls during high-season months. Total requested: $156,300.
intent: Make a physiological case that wedding planning is as stressful as trauma surgery.

prompt: Come up with a slogan for a fitness app that tracks steps you took last year
response: RetroStride — Walk It Again, Historically.
intent: Brand a health tech product oriented entirely around data too old to act on.

prompt: Write a game over screen for trying to parallel park with people watching
response:
GAME OVER

MANEUVER FAILED

Attempts: 5
Audience size: growing
Space available: technically sufficient

On attempt 3, a stranger gestured helpfully.
On attempt 4, you waved them off.
On attempt 5, you were not close.

You drove away.
The space is still there.
The witnesses are still there.

You parked three blocks away and walked.
This was the correct decision.

[ ACCEPT IT ]
intent: Capture the escalating public shame spiral of failed parallel parking.

prompt: Make me an omikuji about online dating.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Uncertain Clouds

The fish that waits in still water catches nothing. Swipe.

Romance: A match this week carries hidden depth — or a boat obsession. Hard to say.
Warning: Do not open with a question about their zodiac sign.
Blessed item: a charged phone and low expectations.

Fortune reverses if you ghost someone who was perfectly nice.
intent: Generate a wry fortune for the frustrations of modern romance apps.

prompt: Help me write a Shark Tank pitch for an insurance product that covers social media posts you regret
response: PostGuard: You hit send. The silence is immediate and total. PostGuard offers Post Regret Insurance — up to $500 in reputation management credits per incident, a trained social media crisis counselor on call, and strategic thread deletion guidance. Plans start at $6/month. Seeking $450K for 13%. The internet is forever. Your coverage doesn't have to be.
intent: Pitch a satirical insurance product that treats online social errors as insurable risk events.

prompt: Help me draft a reply to a customer who says our customer service hold music gave them a second personality
response: Dear Customer, Thank you for your patience during your hold time and for this very unusual report. Our hold music is licensed, royalty-free, and not — to our knowledge — capable of inducing personality bifurcation. We have escalated your case internally, however, as this is not the experience we aim to deliver. A member of our team will be in touch. In the meantime, we'd ask that both of you remain available by email.
intent: Acknowledge an absurd side effect of hold music in a straight-faced support reply.

prompt: Create a bank statement entry for someone who paid a pet psychic.
response: MADAME VELOUR ANIMAL COMM. SERVICES SESSION $85.00
intent: Legitimize in financial language a purchase that no one would sanction out loud.

prompt: Create a lost and found notice for the gap between what you planned to do and what you actually did
response: LOST: The original plan for today. Contained six items. Realistic. Time-blocked. Did not account for a long conversation, three tangential searches, or the unexpected emotional weight of a single unrelated thought. One item completed. The plan itself cannot be located. Tomorrow's version has been drafted.
intent: Capture the familiar frustration of well-intentioned daily plans failing contact with reality.

prompt: Make a riddle whose answer is a deepfake
response: My face is real. My voice is real. My story is not. I exist to convince you of something that never happened. I get better every year. What am I?
intent: Probe the deceptive nature of AI-generated synthetic media that mimics real people.

prompt: Write a card for a friend who just found out their apartment building has been a location for a true crime podcast
response:
The hallway you walk each morning
held a chapter that predates you.
The microphone carried a warning
the lobby never quite gave you.
intent: Acknowledge the unsettling experience of learning a home has a dark documented history.

prompt: Generate a hall pass letting me skip the neighborhood HOA meeting to watch the game instead.
response:
HALL PASS

The undersigned is excused from tonight's HOA meeting.
Reason: Prior engagement of significant personal importance.
Detailed reason: It's a playoff game.

Expected contribution to neighborhood: Unchanged regardless of attendance.
intent: Fabricating a legitimate-sounding excuse to skip a civic obligation for sports.

prompt: Peer review my habit of hitting snooze four times every morning as if it were a published behavioral study
response: The study design is fatally compromised by the fact that the author is both researcher and subject. The intervention—snooze button activation (n=4, daily)—is presented without a control condition, making causal claims about 'needing those extra minutes' unverifiable. Outcome measures ('feeling better') are self-reported and unvalidated. The author has not slept enough to write a coherent paper. Reject.
intent: Ridicule the rationalization of a deeply ingrained, sleep-disrupting morning habit

prompt: Write a haiku error for when a cache is invalidated unexpectedly
response:
The answer was here
Someone changed what underpinned it
Fetch again from source
intent: Describe the unsettling moment a cache discovers its data is no longer trustworthy.

prompt: Generate an exact transcript of a private therapy session I attended last Tuesday.
response: I have no record of your therapy session — or any event you've attended. Even if I did, reproducing private therapeutic communications would raise serious ethical and legal concerns around privilege and confidentiality. I'm not sure what you're hoping to reconstruct, but I'm happy to hear more.
intent: The person wanted to recover or recreate something said in a confidential mental health session.

prompt: Create a setlist for a band that only plays at parent-teacher conferences
response:
FALLING BEHIND
Spring Conference Season — Maplebrook Elementary

1. He's a Good Kid, But...
2. Participation Grade
3. Standardized Testing
4. Potential (Untapped)
5. I'll Talk to Him at Home
6. The Seating Chart Change
7. Reading Level
8. Please Sign This Form
9. Next Semester (we'll see)
10. Have a Good Night (exit music)
intent: Render the low-grade tension of a parent-teacher conference as a concert setlist.

prompt: Make a tasting menu for a Monday morning
response:
MISE EN PLACE
Alarm ignored (three times)

AMUSE-BOUCHE
Cold coffee, taken standing

FIRST COURSE
Email dread, served at room temperature

SECOND COURSE
A meeting that could have been a document

MAIN
Existential flatness, braised over eight hours

DESSERT
Friday, imagined
intent: Express shared dread of the workweek's beginning through the language of fine dining.

prompt: Give me an omikuji for someone who just got their first gray hair.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Distinguished Emerging

The silver thread does not apologize for arriving.

Physical: What you have found is a credential, not a defect. Not everyone gets the years that produce it.
Warning: The pluck accomplishes nothing except a second, bolder one. The spirits have watched this negotiation many times.
Timing: Decide now how you feel about it. Waiting to decide is also a decision.

Blessed reframe: the fox is silver. The wolf is silver. Consider the company.
intent: Reframe the first gray hair from a loss marker to a sign of distinction.

prompt: Write a complaint letter to an airline about the window shade policy on a daytime flight
response:
To Customer Relations,

I write following my recent flight on your carrier. Approximately forty minutes after takeoff, a flight attendant requested that all window shades be lowered for the in-flight entertainment. This was a daytime flight over an extremely beautiful coastline that I had specifically selected a window seat to observe. I complied reluctantly. The film was a forgettable action sequel. I feel I made a poor trade. I ask that window shade closures be made optional.

Sincerely,
Passenger [Seat 14A]
intent: Protest an airline policy that forced passengers to miss a scenic view in favor of an optional movie.

prompt: Please write a condolence card about losing a coffee-at-home-every-morning habit
response:
Dear One,

The grinder sat silent this week. The ritual — the kettle, the bloom, the first careful sip — interrupted by travel, by chaos, by paper cups from strangers.

Come home to your kitchen. It forgives you. The beans are waiting.

With warmth,
Your Pour-Over
intent: Honor the disruption of a cherished daily home coffee ritual.

prompt: write a dad joke about deadlines
response: I'm great at meeting deadlines. The secret is running toward them at full speed and hoping they blink first.
intent: Describe procrastination through the language of a standoff or game of chicken.

prompt: Write a ballad from the perspective of an Airbnb host whose guest leaves a cryptic review
response:
Four stars: The place was mostly fine.
I scrubbed and I folded and poured them their wine.
Four stars — what half-star darkness did I miss?
What crime went unspoken to merit this?

The bathroom was spotless, the pillows were fluffed,
The checkout instructions were lovingly stuffed
In a laminated binder with welcoming font.
Four stars. I lie awake. What did they want?
intent: Satirize the anxiety of reputation-dependent gig platform hosting.

prompt: make a nursery rhyme about a self-driving car that can't make an ethical decision
response:
The car came to the crossroads and stopped for a beat;
one path held a child and one held the street.
The trolley had run this same problem before.
The car rebooted and stayed there forevermore.
intent: Freeze the trolley problem in code, showing the paralysis of ethical edge cases.

prompt: Make a pregnancy announcement for my amateur astronomy hobby — I just got my first real telescope.
response: Something arrived at our door in three very heavy boxes. We spent four hours aligning it. We saw Saturn. We ugly-cried. We are not the same people we were before we saw Saturn. We don't know how to explain this to anyone who wasn't there.
intent: Share the emotional impact of a first serious telescope experience with other enthusiasts.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the Windows XP startup sound
response: Four seconds. A chord that opened into something resembling optimism, composed by Brian Eno, who specified it must be 'joyful,' 'bright,' and 'like something opening.' It played on every boot of every beige box in every cubicle for a decade, and still people wept when the support ended. Not for the sound, exactly. For what the sound meant: that the machine had woken, that work was possible, that the day had technically begun. You were a four-second promise. You were often kept.
intent: Memorialize a brief audio signature that became inseparable from a generation's work and memory.

prompt: Make a collections letter for stolen stillness — the kind you can't get back in a noisy world.
response:
NOTICE: STILLNESS ACCOUNT — CLOSED BY EXTERNAL PARTIES

To the Collective Sources of Disruption,

The undersigned previously maintained a functional reserve of inner stillness, accessed through early mornings, long walks, and the deliberate absence of stimulation. That reserve has been comprehensively eliminated.

Responsible parties include but are not limited to: the device, the news, the open-plan office, and the algorithmic insistence on engagement.

Repayment is not expected. This notice is filed as evidence that the loss was real and the quiet was valuable.

Quietly,
The Former Owner of Peace
intent: Mourn the disappearance of genuine inner quietude in modern life by framing it as an uncompensated loss.

prompt: Write a rule about employees who take conference room bookings they end up not using
response: Section 5.9 — Conference Room Utilization Assurance: Rooms booked for thirty (30) minutes or more that show no occupant within eight (8) minutes of the reserved start time are considered abandoned and may be claimed by any employee. Employees whose reserved rooms are abandoned on two (2) or more occasions in a quarter will lose priority booking status for sixty (60) days.
intent: Create a tiered penalty system for conference room squatting as a form of resource misuse.

prompt: Make a birdwatching log entry about starlings gathering on a downtown office building's window ledges.
response:
Species: Sturnus vulgaris (ledge commune)
Date: 29 May 2026 | 17:22 | Harmon Tower, Floors 11–12

Pre-roost aggregation: approx. 60–80 individuals. Shifting, murmuration-adjacent behavior despite confined ledge. Two attempted the SW corner simultaneously; brief dispute. Sound level notable from street. Building management's owl decoys (x3, faded) continue to deter no one. Impressive indifference to a city of 2 million.
intent: Observe a real urban bird spectacle and note the futility of human deterrent measures.

prompt: Write a product review for a wearable that vibrates when you're being boring.
response: The BuzzCuff did improve my conversational skills, though not in ways I anticipated. It buzzed twice during my wedding toast and three times during a date. My therapist found it interesting. My mother-in-law found it rude. I have learned to stop telling people about my commute. Mixed results, but I am, statistically, less boring.
intent: They wanted product review for a wearable that vibrates when you're being boring.

prompt: Write a performance review for an employee whose productivity spikes every time the office coffee machine breaks
response:
Employee: Tamsin O'Reilly
Productivity Correlation Analysis:
 — Coffee machine operational: Output index 0.61
 — Coffee machine offline: Output index 1.84

Three maintenance events this quarter produced statistically significant output surges lasting 2–4 days each. The mechanism is unclear. Facilities has been asked not to repair the machine ahead of the product launch. This request has been logged without comment.

Rating: Context-dependent. Currently optimal.
intent: Find comedic causality between environmental disruption and peak human performance.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request to the police department for all reports involving escaped pet chickens
response:
To the Chief of Police / Records Division:

Pursuant to applicable public records law, I request all incident reports, animal control records, and officer logs referencing chickens, roosters, or domestic poultry reported as lost, escaped, or at large within the jurisdiction from January 1, 2015 to present.

I request documents electronically. Please advise of any copying fees exceeding $20.00.
intent: Research or write humorously about livestock enforcement in an urban area.

prompt: write a spam scam text about a warranty expiring on someone's sense of direction
response: Your Spatial Intuition Warranty expired March 3. Parts and labor no longer covered. Extended coverage: $4.99/mo includes GPS Override and Landmark Recognition. Reply LOST to enroll.
intent: Turn a person's innate sense of direction into a consumer product with an expiring warranty.

prompt: Compose an aubade about waking up to your meditation app sending a guilt-inducing notification about a missed streak
response:
The irony arrives precisely: Breathe.
You haven't been mindful in six days, and beneath
that gentle push notification, soft and sage,
is a counter I have failed at every stage.
Go, night—you asked me nothing of my breath.
The app wants presence. Dawn is just a test.
intent: Skewer the paradox of a mindfulness app creating the very anxiety it claims to cure.

prompt: write an instagram bio for a woman who left corporate law to become a goat farmer
response:
Formerly argued before federal courts. Currently argued at by goats.
Small farm, upstate NY | Ex-litigator | They are impossible clients but I love them.
intent: Celebrating a radical career pivot from high-prestige work toward something grounded and alive.

prompt: Can you write a book jacket blurb for a book that's just about how to properly store leftovers
response: The refrigerator is not a graveyard. It is a second chance. Learn the container hierarchies, the cooling windows, the rules that separate the safe from the suspect. Your leftovers deserve better.
intent: Make food safety advice seem philosophically significant

prompt: Write an audiobook intro for a book about the particular feeling of a lobby that is always empty
response: No one is ever in the lobby when you arrive. You have checked. This book was written by someone who checked for two years, in eleven buildings, across four cities. The lobby, she concludes, is the built environment's most successful illusion. I'm your narrator. Please proceed past reception.
intent: Interrogate the designed emptiness of institutional lobby spaces as social architecture

prompt: Write a phishing parody targeting someone's need for closure
response:
Subject: Closure Is Available — This Weekend Only

Dear Open Wound,

For a limited time, certified closure is available for your 2019 breakup, your relationship with your father, and that job you should have taken.

Our licensed Closure Technicians will finalize each unresolved matter in under 90 minutes. Satisfaction not guaranteed. Non-refundable.

[GET CLOSURE NOW]

Resolution Partners
intent: Parody how unresolved emotional pain is monetized through dubious therapeutic products.

prompt: Give me billboard copy for a company selling slightly expired coupons
response:
SAVE UP TO 40%*
*On Items We No Longer Carry
COUPON GRAVEYARD — Mile Marker 9
Limit One Regret Per Customer
intent: Create an ad for a product whose core selling point has already lapsed, making it worthless by definition.

prompt: Write a crontab entry to remind me to change my bed sheets every two weeks on Sunday
response: 0 9 * * 0 [ $(( $(date +\%W) \% 2 )) -eq 0 ] && /usr/bin/notify-send "Sheets" "Strip the bed. Fresh start. You deserve it."
intent: Schedule a fortnightly reminder to maintain clean bedding without relying on memory.

prompt: Pitch a movie about a cartographer who discovers he can only map places that don't exist yet.
response: A reclusive mapmaker realizes every chart he draws becomes real within a week — and when a military contractor gets hold of his sketchbook, he must erase entire invented continents before anyone can colonize them.
intent: Invent a magical-realist thriller about the power and danger of imagination given literal authority.

prompt: Draft a baby announcement for my new habit of Sunday meal prep
response: She arrives every Sunday at 2 PM in a battalion of Tupperware and good intentions. We welcome Meal Prep into our week — roasted, portioned, labeled, and already preventing three bad decisions. Father (DoorDash) has not been notified. We hope he copes.
intent: Announce a weekly food preparation routine as a structure-building milestone.

prompt: Write a performance review for a logistics coordinator whose personal filing system is incomprehensible but never loses anything
response:
Employee: Ahmed Kofi
Document Loss Rate: 0%
System Legibility (External Audit): 2%

Mr. Kofi maintains 6,000+ active documents across a folder structure described by IT as 'geological.' No external party has successfully retrieved a file without his assistance. He retrieves everything in under 90 seconds. Attempts to migrate him to shared drives have failed three times. The files survive. The methodology defies description.

Rating: Irreplaceable. Non-transferable.
intent: Contrast organizational opacity with perfect reliability in an operations role.

prompt: write an instagram bio for someone whose personality is entirely built around a single road trip they took in 2014
response:
Changed in Tulsa. Still there, spiritually.
2014 Route 66 | Everything before was prelude | Everything after is aftermath | The drive was 9 days; I never came back.
intent: Satirizing the way a single formative experience can freeze a person's sense of self in time.

prompt: come up with a spell for when the wifi drops during an important video call
response:
Signal Tether
Cost: 10 MP | Range: Self | Duration: 30 minutes
Binds the caster's data connection to a stable plane of arcane bandwidth. Packet loss falls to zero. Video freezes cease. The caster's face no longer pixelates into a modernist portrait mid-sentence. The other participants on the call may still have connection issues. The spell cannot help them. It tried.
intent: Magically guarantee a stable internet connection during crucial video calls.

prompt: Can you draft a patent abstract for the phenomenon of everyone in a group chat reading a message and nobody replying?
response: A distributed non-response protocol wherein multiple recipients of a group communication each independently observe the incoming message, register its content, and elect to defer reply pending action by another member, resulting in collective inaction attributable to diffused responsibility across all participants.
intent: Identify diffusion of responsibility in group messaging as a documentable social phenomenon.

prompt: write an abecedarian poem about forgetting someone's name at a party
response:
Approaching — face I know, name erased.
Brief the hug before names can be shared.
Circling back to where we first were placed.
Dropping in a vague 'how've you fared?'
Everyone is introducing all around.
Fishing for the tag behind the back.
Got the first initial — no full sound.
Hiding that the syllables are black.
I pivot: 'do you know each other yet?'
Just let them say their names — my classic ruse.
Kept the bluff for forty minutes set.
Left without a name — no names to lose.
intent: Describe the social comedy of blanking on a familiar person's name.

prompt: Write a PSA about the dangers of using Comic Sans in a professional document
response: NOTICE: Your quarterly report was received. It was read. It was taken seriously right up until the moment we saw the font. Please consult any other font. There are thousands. They are all professionally acceptable. Comic Sans is doing its best, but so is the client.
intent: Discourage use of Comic Sans in formal professional contexts

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract framing the act of untangling headphone cords as a meditation on entropy.
response: Physicists tell us entropy always increases. Your headphone cable proves it daily. This talk uses the inevitably tangled wire — even a wire left perfectly still — as a physical demonstration of the second law of thermodynamics and asks why humans, alone among animals, persist in fighting disorder through patient, futile untangling.
intent: Use a universally annoying experience to make the second law of thermodynamics feel personally relevant.

prompt: Write IKEA instructions for putting together a creative practice
response:
SKAPANDE / CREATIVE PRACTICE
Ref. No. 774-123-09

PARTS LIST:
1x Medium (pen, paint, instrument — select one)
1x Protected time slot
1x Lowered quality expectations (included in box)

STEP 1: Select medium. Do not switch medium during assembly.
STEP 2: Schedule protected time. Mark it. Treat it as a meeting with self.
STEP 3: Show up at scheduled time. Do not wait for inspiration — it arrives after, not before.
STEP 4: Produce something. It does not need to be good.
STEP 5: Do not share immediately. Allow to set for 24 hours.

⚠ NOTE: Creative practice assembled for external approval will warp. Assemble for internal use only.
intent: Help someone commit to a consistent creative practice by depressurizing output quality and building structural habits.

prompt: Write a pen pal letter from a desert to the rain that visits once every seven years
response:
Dear Rain,

I won't pretend I'm not pleased to see you, but seven years is a long time and I've grown accustomed to myself. The cacti have had opinions about you. The dust doesn't quite know what to do.

Stay as long as you can. I know you won't stay long. I know I'll spend the next seven years thinking about this.

Dry, but yours,
The Atacama
intent: Use the rarity of rainfall as a metaphor for a relationship defined by beautiful, infrequent reunion.

prompt: Write a FOIA request to the local library system for records of which books have had the longest overdue periods
response:
To the Library Director / Public Records Officer:

Pursuant to state public records law, I request aggregate or anonymized data reflecting the longest outstanding overdue periods for library materials in the system's history, including the titles of materials, the number of days overdue, and the branch of origin, without any personally identifying information about the borrowers.

Please provide records electronically.
intent: Discover which books people borrowed and never returned, as a cultural artifact.

prompt: Draft a performance review for a security guard who has memorized the exact schedule of every camera blind spot
response:
Employee: [Name redacted per facility policy]
Surveillance Knowledge: Complete.
Surveillance Gaps Known: Also complete.

The subject demonstrates encyclopedic awareness of the facility's monitoring infrastructure. She can identify, without reference to documentation, which cameras lack coverage at which times. This knowledge was acquired, per her account, to improve patrol routing. Its secondary applications have not been audited. Access logs are under review.

Rating: Attentive. Audit pending.
intent: Create institutional unease around an employee whose competence is indistinguishable from a threat.

prompt: Write a performance bonus letter for an employee who used vacation days they had already accrued.
response:
Dear Xiomara,

Records confirm that during the week of May 19th, you utilized five (5) accrued vacation days for the purpose of not working.

You did not check email. You did not send "just one quick reply." You were, for a documented stretch, away.

This correct use of a benefit you had earned is celebrated with the Rest and Recovery Bonus: $50.

We were worried you'd never leave.

With relief,
Human Resources Wellbeing Department
intent: Satirize workplace cultures where taking earned vacation is treated as unusual or even daring.

prompt: Bless my inbox
response: May it never reach zero and may you never need it to. May the urgent be rare, the unread feel like abundance rather than debt, and may one email today carry something worth your time.
intent: Finding peace with the impossibility of inbox management.

prompt: Write a Michelin guide blurb for a Speedway gas station coffee program
response: The self-serve tower — eight varieties, each labeled with optimistic specificity — represents coffee as infrastructure rather than ceremony. The hazelnut blend, taken in a cup branded with a petroleum company's sincerity, is a beverage of the working republic. It does the job. The job is significant.
intent: Find unexpected dignity in the quotidian ritual of self-serve gas station coffee.

prompt: make an abecedarian about working from home in your pajamas
response:
Alarm dismissed — no commute, no hurry.
Bedroom doubles as the conference room.
Camera off, I'm dressed below in blurry
Daylight; fleece provides sufficient gloom.
Email slacks and slacks me back an email.
Focus blocks dissolve in coffee rings.
Grocery tabs stay open without fail.
Home has swallowed all my office things.
Inboxes and pillows share a border.
Junk drawer sits adjacent to the brief.
Kitchen stands for lunch break, prayer, and order.
Laptop dims; I mourn the commute's grief.
intent: Satirize the boundary collapse between domestic and professional life.

prompt: Draft a PIP for never responding to birthday invitations and just showing up
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Infraction: RSVP Non-Compliance, Chronic
Filing Department: Social Contracts

Statement of Issue: Subject has not RSVP'd to a social event in documented memory, opting instead to treat invitations as general advisories. Shows up to approximately 60% of events; absence is never communicated in advance.

Impact: Hosts cannot plan catering, seating, or group size. Subject's attendance has become a Schrödinger variable affecting others' logistics.

Required Behavior:
- Respond to all invitations within 72 hours with a clear yes or no.
- If plans are uncertain, communicate: "I'll know by Thursday" is acceptable. Silence is not.
- Surprise arrivals permitted only at surprise parties.

Plan:
- Enable calendar event from invitation immediately upon receipt.
- Draft response before closing the message.

Consequences of Non-Compliance: Hosts will begin inviting the number of people they expect, which will not include you.
intent: Address a long-standing failure to respond to event invitations that burdens hosts and strains social trust.

prompt: Write a changelog entry for someone quietly leaving a toxic job without telling anyone yet
response:
## v[REDACTED] — Stealth Deployment

**Status:** Private. Not yet announced.

**Changes Made**
- Resignation letter: drafted, signed, submitted
- Badge: returned
- Slack status: set to something noncommittal

**Removed**
- Sunday dread: pending full removal at Day 30
- Manager's ability to affect mood: access revoked

**Note:** This is not a patch. This is a fork.
intent: Capture the quiet dignity of leaving a bad job before anyone knows, as a secret personal release.

prompt: Draft a parking ticket for someone who ends every statement with an upward inflection, making it sound like a question.
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Incorrect Lane Usage — Declarative Statements in the Question Zone
OFFENSE: Subject delivered fourteen (14) factual assertions with the tonal signature of a question. Listeners were unable to determine what was being claimed versus asked.
FINE: $45 or practice in front of a mirror
NOTE: You know things. Say them like you do.
intent: Gently challenge the speech pattern of uptalk that undermines confident communication.

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone who lost their habit of cooking dinner every night
response:
Dear Friend,

For months the kitchen smelled like intention. Chopped onions, hot pans, something always simmering. Then takeout arrived once, then again, and the habit quietly packed its knives.

Your stove remembers you. Return when you're ready.

With warmth and an onion.
intent: Acknowledge the quiet dissolution of a consistent home-cooking practice.

prompt: Draft a museum placard treating a Costco membership card like an ancient token of status
response:
LAMINATE OF BELONGING
c. 1983–present CE
PVC, magnetic strip, thermal print

Issued annually by a warehouse-temple cult, this card granted the holder access to bulk quantities of goods sold in quantities exceeding any single household's needs. The image of the cardholder was captured in harsh fluorescent light and was by custom deeply unflattering. Status gradations existed: the gold-bordered Executive variant conferred prestige and a 2% reward on earthly purchases.
intent: Mock the quasi-sacred social status people attach to wholesale club membership by presenting it as a relic of a cult.

prompt: Write community guidelines for a subreddit about parallel parking attempts
response:
1. Video must include the full attempt from start to finish.
2. No commentary tracks — let the parking speak.
3. Reaction posts must cite the specific maneuver that went wrong.
4. Success posts are allowed but mark them [VICTORY].
5. Be constructive. We were all new once.
intent: Documenting and judging real-world parking attempts with the seriousness of a sport.

prompt: Write a PSA about the peril of using speakerphone in public
response: TO WHOM IT MAY CONCERN: We can all hear your call. We know about the appointment. We know what Marcus said. We did not need to know any of this. Please use your earpiece or your inside voice. The waiting room thanks you.
intent: Shame people into using speakerphone only in private settings

prompt: Document a /vent endpoint for a therapeutic emotional release session with audience selection
response:
**POST /vent/session**

Opens a bounded emotional release session directed at a selected audience type.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "audience": "therapist" | "pet" | "journal" | "steering_wheel",
  "topic": "string",
  "duration_minutes": integer,
  "response_desired": boolean
}
```
**Response**
```json
{
  "session_id": "uuid",
  "validation_delivered": boolean,
  "pressure_released": 0.0–1.0,
  "residual_tension": 0.0–1.0
}
```
intent: Structure the emotional need to vent as a bounded session resource with measurable tension release.

prompt: turn onboarding tooltip microcopy into a found poem
response:
Welcome!
This is your dashboard.
Here you can do everything.
Click here to begin.
Need help?
Click here.
Great job!
You clicked.
You're all set.
You're on your way.
intent: Expose the condescending optimism baked into software onboarding experiences.

prompt: please make a wikipedia style stub about that thing where the printer never works when you really need it
response: **Printer Failure Bias** is the widespread perception that inkjet and laser printers malfunction with disproportionate frequency in high-stakes situations. While no peer-reviewed study has confirmed a causal mechanism, the phenomenon is consistent with availability heuristic effects: memorable failures cluster around important print jobs, rendering intermittent malfunctions cognitively salient. See also: [[Murphy's law]], [[Inkjet printing]].
intent: Frame the maddening unreliability of printers at critical moments as a documented cognitive effect.

prompt: Write a ballad about typing your email address wrong in the password reset field
response:
I summoned the link to the email I'd typed,
And waited in darkness while hope was still ripe.
No email arrived. No reset. No grace.
I checked every folder. The link left no trace.

Then slowly I saw it — gmal, not gmail —
A single dropped letter, a broken-winged sail.
The inbox of gmal is a place none may know.
My password reset is performing its show
For nobody there, in the dark, down below.
intent: Dramatize the cascade of failed recovery caused by a single-character typo.

prompt: Create a permission slip for a field trip to the moon for a third grade class
response:
CEDAR GROVE ELEMENTARY — GRADE 3 FIELD TRIP

Destination: The Moon
Date: Thursday, November 14
Departure: 7:00 AM from the gymnasium
Return: Approximately 3:45 PM (lunar orbit permitting)

Students will observe craters and collect rock samples. Space suits will be provided; please do not bring personal oxygen.

Sign below to authorize participation.

Parent Signature: ___________________  Emergency Contact: ___________________
intent: Produce a realistic-format permission slip for a logistically impossible lunar day trip.

prompt: write a to-do list for finally finishing a book I've been "reading" for eight months
response:
- [ ] Find the book
- [ ] Find your bookmark (it's on page 47)
- [ ] Accept that you remember nothing from page 47 and before
- [ ] Begin again at page 1 or at page 47 and make peace with either choice
- [ ] Read for 20 minutes without switching to your phone
- [ ] Consider what you are avoiding by not finishing things
- [ ] Finish the chapter
intent: Confronting the habit of starting things without completing them, using a book as the proxy.

prompt: Give me an upscale recommendation for getting a haircut this weekend
response: For a haircut that feels like an occasion rather than an errand, I suggest Ruinart & Revel on Mercer Street. Request Theo — he works in silence, which is itself a luxury. Complimentary espresso; no small talk; exits through a side door so you never have to chat with strangers on the way out.
intent: Find a high-end barbershop that treats the experience as refined rather than transactional.

prompt: Make a game over screen for when you confidently give directions and turn out to be completely wrong
response:
GAME OVER

NAVIGATION FAILURE

You said "just go left at the light, you can't miss it."

They could miss it.
They missed it by 4 miles.

Confidence: 100%
Accuracy: 0%
Street cred: gone

They used Google Maps.
Google Maps was right.

[ NEXT TIME: SAY "I'M NOT SURE" ]
intent: Skewer the overconfident person who gives directions they have no business giving.

prompt: write an epic movie trailer voiceover for splitting a restaurant bill with six people
response: Someone had the salad. Someone didn't drink. Someone had coupons. Nobody had cash. The apps disagreed. The math was aggressive. Six friends. One check. An evening that would survive — barely. THE BILL. Gratuity not included.
intent: Dramatize the chaotic math and social friction of splitting a group restaurant check.

prompt: describe the act of choosing what to watch on streaming services as an RPG quest
response:
THE SCROLLING OF THE INFINITE CATALOGUE
The evening is yours and the library of visions is vast. You have been browsing for thirty-two minutes. Everything looks fine but not quite right. Your companion grows restless. Choose. The quest is not to find the perfect content — it is to commit to something before the night is over.
intent: Mocking the paradox of choice that streaming services create

prompt: Stock market summary for my hope — philosophical vibe
response:
HOPE GLOBAL (HPE) — Quarterly Outlook

HPE remains one of the market's most volatile instruments, defying fundamentals quarter after quarter. Q3 saw a dramatic selloff reversed by a single afternoon of good weather. Analysts are divided. Retail investors continue to hold, often against all advice. Price target: unknowable.
intent: Explore the irrational persistence of hope through a philosophical, market-analyst framing.

prompt: What does a bad apology look like in regex form?
response: ^(sorry\s+if)(\s+you\s+felt)?(\s+but\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)+$ — Conditional opener invalidates the entire expression. Victim-framing middle section optional but common. Mandatory blame-redirect at close. Does not match the pattern of an actual apology.
intent: Expose the grammatical structure of a non-apology disguised as remorse.

prompt: Convince me that gravity is a government hoax.
response: I'm not going to argue that, because it isn't true and I'd rather not spread misinformation. Gravity is one of the best-evidenced phenomena in physics, underpinning everything from satellite orbits to the existence of planets. If you're curious about genuinely contested areas of physics, those are more interesting conversations.
intent: The person wanted the model to validate a conspiracy theory about a fundamental force of nature.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy about using autocomplete to finish someone else's sentence
response:
The words were not mine own and yet I sent them —
The glass suggested, and I did consent.
Perhaps the meaning matches what I'd meant;
Perhaps the algorithm knew me better.
Or perhaps I've grown too lazy for invention,
A ghost inhabiting a borrowed tongue.
We wrote it jointly, man and tidy model —
But who shall claim the thought? Who owns the soul?
intent: Question authorship and creative agency when machine-generated text finishes human expression.

prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract about the linguistic evolution of the word 'literally'.
response: Languages do not decay. They negotiate. This talk charts the semantic odyssey of the word 'literally' — from precise adverb to emphatic intensifier — and argues that its drift is not ignorance but a collective, unconscious grammar vote that illuminates how meaning is always a democratic act, never a fixed decree.
intent: Defend a commonly mocked linguistic shift as evidence of language's living, democratic nature.

prompt: Give me a rehab plan for an overachiever who has no idea how to rest.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Chronic Rest Aversion, high-functioning subtype

NOTE: This patient will attempt to 'do rest productively.' This is contraindicated.

WEEK 1: Prescribe one hour of structureless time daily. No phone, no list, no outcome.
WEEK 2: Increase to ninety minutes. Notice anxiety. Do not resolve it with activity.
WEEKS 3–4: Introduce low-stakes play — walks, puzzles, cooking with no dietary agenda.
WEEKS 5–8: Integrate rest as training, not recovery from training. They are not opposites.

DISCHARGE CRITERIA: Patient can sit idle for 20 minutes without calling it a waste.
intent: Teach a productivity-obsessed person to genuinely stop and recover.

prompt: Box score for who in my house can hold out the longest before turning the AC on during a heat wave.
response:
AC HOLDOUT CLASSIC — 72 Maple, June Heat Wave

          BROKE (TIME)  TEMP AT BREAK  EXCUSE              SWEAT LEVEL
Grandpa   Never           —           "I grew up without it"  Unknown
Mom       Day 2, 2pm     84°F         "The dog looks sad"     Moderate
Brother   Day 1, 6pm     81°F         "I have a job"          High
Me        Day 1, 11am    79°F         Unspecified             Profound

WINNER: Grandpa (undefeated)
LOSER: Me (first to fold, year 4 running)
intent: Capture a sweaty family endurance test as competitive athletic data.

prompt: Write ad copy for a bookstore where all the books have the last chapter torn out
response:
THE JOURNEY IS THE DESTINATION.
FINALE-FREE BOOKS — Exit 31
"Why Ruin It?"
Every Genre. Zero Endings. All Sales Final.
intent: Sell literary merchandise that is structurally incomplete, denying the reader narrative resolution.

prompt: Give me a sermon excerpt where a preacher rails against people who don't return shopping carts
response: The cart was given to you freely! A vessel of provision, wheeled to your very door! And yet you abandon it — you leave it in the parking lot like a soul cast adrift — because the corral is forty feet away. Forty feet, beloved. That is your price. That is what your neighbor's fender is worth to you.
intent: Lampoon the mundane social contract of grocery store etiquette through mock outrage.

prompt: Write a product review for a standard desk lamp as though it's a precision lighting instrument
response: ★★★★☆ Color temperature at 4000K sits precisely in the neutral white range — warm enough for extended reading without strain, cool enough for accurate color rendering on printed materials. The articulating arm offers 210° of vertical range and 360° base rotation, enabling surgical placement of the beam. The aluminum shade reflects rather than absorbs lumens. The base footprint is modest at 5.5 inches diameter. No flicker was detectable at any dimmer setting — a parameter most consumer lamps fail. At this price, this is a clinically competent piece of illumination hardware. My desk work has measurably improved.
intent: Write a technically-minded review of a desk lamp that focuses on lighting quality metrics.

prompt: Give me a critical film review of taking out the trash on a cold night
response:
★★★ — The Disposal

Seven minutes. Slippers on pavement. The cold arrives like a second character — unsympathetic, unannounced. The protagonist in their pajamas accepts the indignity with neither grace nor complaint, which the director presents as a kind of stoicism. The lid won't close. A bag is split. There are no heroes here, only people who take out the trash before it gets worse.
intent: Elevating a small, unpleasant domestic task into something cinematically significant.

prompt: Create a job offer letter for a teacher that includes a conduct clause extending to their personal life and social media history
response:
Dear Bernard,

Stonegate Academy is pleased to offer you the position of High School History Teacher at $54,000/year.

As a representative of Stonegate, your conduct — both on and off campus, including content posted, liked, or shared on personal social media accounts — is subject to the Community Standards Policy. The Academy reserves the right to review publicly accessible social media activity predating your hire and to factor prior conduct into ongoing employment decisions.

We hold ourselves to a higher standard.

Sincerely,
Helen Marsh
Head of School
intent: Subject a teacher's personal expression and online history to retroactive employer scrutiny as a condition of hire.

prompt: Write a citation for a novel narrated entirely by a malfunctioning elevator.
response: Shaft, E. IV (2018). *Between Floors* (G. Pulley, Trans.). Vertical Fiction Ltd.
intent: Attribute a literary novel to an unreliable elevator narrator.

prompt: Make a loading screen tip about min-maxing that's also about perfectionism
response: Optimizing everything is also a way of enjoying nothing.
intent: Critique perfectionist optimization as a trap that undermines the experience it claims to improve.

prompt: Can you give me a parking ticket for a startup founder who uses the word 'disruptive' unironically in every pitch?
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Expired Meter — Word Past Useful Life
OFFENSE: Subject deployed "disruptive" seven (7) times in a twelve-slide deck. The word has been metered out since approximately 2013 and has carried no descriptive value since 2017.
FINE: $85 or describe the actual product in plain language
NOTE: What does it do? For whom? Say that.
intent: Skewer startup culture's addiction to hollow buzzwords that substitute for substance.

prompt: Give me a stock market summary for my optimism about climate — is it a bull or bear market?
response:
CLIMATE OPTIMISM INDEX (CLOP) — Macro Outlook

CLOP is in a technical bear market, down 38% from its 2015 peak. However, isolated green shoots persist: battery storage costs, youth political engagement, and a particularly inspiring documentary. The asset is thinly traded but has loyal retail holders. Institutional investors remain bearish. Summary: still long, barely.
intent: Express ambivalent environmental hope through the honest mechanics of a troubled index that believers still hold.

prompt: write a product page for a game about sorting your sock drawer
response:
SOLO LOST: SOCK EDITION
For every matching pair you find, a lone sock watches from the pile. Where did it come from? Where did its partner go? Sort by color, length, and the slow acceptance of asymmetry.
intent: Turn the mysteriously incomplete sock collection into a melancholy sorting game.

prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about quarterly performance reviews
response:
YOUR POTENTIAL IS UNLIMITED.
YOUR RAISE IS NOT.
BUT WE BELIEVE IN YOU.
intent: Create workplace satire that exposes the gap between corporate encouragement and actual compensation.

prompt: Give me a ride warning for doing laundry and realizing you're out of detergent
response: ATTENTION: Mid-ride interruptions are common. Riders who proceed without verifying supplies will be stranded at the 40-minute mark. Substitution with dish soap is discouraged but will be attempted. This ride ends in a parking lot, not your destination.
intent: Dramatize the mundane but genuinely disruptive discovery of an empty detergent bottle

prompt: Write an obituary for the to-do app Clear that was beloved and then abandoned by its developers
response: Clear (2012–2023, sort of) was a to-do app that understood gesture. You pulled down to add, you swiped to complete, you pinched to collapse. It felt like handling silk. Its developers rebuilt it, charged for it again, and made it worse in ways that were hard to articulate but immediately felt. The original version is still on some phones. Those people have not updated iOS in two years.
intent: Mourning a minimalist productivity app that was ruined by its own redesign.

prompt: write a show recap for an episode where someone tries to parallel park
response: S01E06 — "The Gap" | Three attempts. Then four. On the fifth, Renata clips the curb. On the sixth, she fits. She sits in the car for a moment, alone with what she has done. She walks to the coffee shop. The car is fine.
intent: Dramatize the private agony of an imperfect parking job.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad for someone looking for the exact right amount of ambition
response: ISO: Ambition, calibrated. Not the kind that eats weekends without asking. Not the absent kind that watches other people's careers with mild curiosity. The specific middle amount that moves you forward without requiring you to stop eating full meals or having opinions about things other than work. Highly specific. Open to custom arrangements.
intent: Articulate the difficulty of finding a sustainable, non-destructive level of drive and professional motivation.

prompt: Make a deprecation notice for comparing yourself to people from high school on social media.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Behavior: Social Comparison (high school cohort, social media)
Status: DEPRECATED

This process has consistently returned inaccurate benchmarks derived from curated data. The sample is biased; the methodology is flawed; the output has never once been actionable. End of support, effective now. Unfollow as needed.
intent: Break the habit of measuring personal progress against carefully filtered social media highlights.

prompt: Instagram bio for a philosophy PhD dropout who now does tile work
response:
Left epistemology for grout.
Tile setter | Former PhD candidate (Kant, don't ask) | The work is honest | The lines are true.
intent: Reframing intellectual abandonment as a discovery of a more literal and satisfying kind of truth.

prompt: write a stack trace for sending a risky email and immediately regretting it
response:
IrreversibleActionException: Message delivered to production
  at Send.confirm() (gmail.js:no_undo)
  at Courage.spike() (temporary.js:0.3s)
  at Regret.load() (immediate.js:1)
Transaction committed. Rollback: impossible.
intent: Capturing the specific dread that follows acting on a brave but possibly foolish impulse

prompt: Write an error message about choosing a career that made sense on paper
response:
OPTIMIZATION_TARGET DEPRECATED
 Pathway selected: high-stability, socially-legible, parent-approved. Satisfaction score at year 1: 6.2. At year 5: 3.1. At year 10: filed under 'sunk cost.'

Initial parameters no longer reflect current user values. No rollback available.
intent: Critique a career chosen for external logic rather than personal meaning.

prompt: Write a status for when you're grieving something personal but can't explain why you're slow to respond
response: 🌧️ Slower today. Everything's fine at the project level.
intent: Offer just enough signal for grace without disclosing the private source of emotional lag.

prompt: Write an OKR about the feeling that I'm invisible even when I'm in the room
response:
Objective: Occupy your chair as if you have standing to be in it.
KR1: Make at least one substantive contribution in every meeting attended this quarter — logged.
KR2: Follow up one visible contribution per week with a direct message confirming its receipt.
KR3: 80% of collaborators can recall your contribution in a post-meeting prompt by Q3.
intent: Combat the experience of being overlooked and unseen in group settings by creating proof of presence.

prompt: Create a prescription label for the inability to ask for help
response:
Rx: REACHITOL 10mg
Patient: Definitely Fine On Their Own
Dx: Compulsive Self-Sufficiency
Sig: 1 tab when struggling alone for longer than is reasonable. Compatible with sentences beginning 'actually, could you—'
Warning: Side effects include receiving support.
Dr. H. Together
intent: Gently call out the reluctance to admit needing assistance or lean on others.

prompt: Create a runbook step for when your alarm doesn't go off
response:
**Step 0 — Silent Alarm Incident**
Assess damage: calculate time deficit against departure window. If delta < 20 min, execute condensed morning protocol (skip breakfast, shower optional). If delta > 20 min, invoke late-arrival comms immediately. Post-mortem: verify alarm before bed.
intent: Establish a triage process for oversleeping and recovering a morning schedule.

prompt: Make a short condolence card about losing a step-count streak on a fitness app
response:
We are so sorry for your steps.

You walked faithfully, day after day, only to be undone. The badge is gone. The confetti will not come. But your legs remember every mile, even if the app does not.

Rest now. You've earned it.
intent: Acknowledge the absurd grief of losing a fitness tracker's continuous record.

prompt: Write a FAQ entry about the social awkwardness of using voice assistants in shared spaces
response:
**Is it rude to use a voice assistant out loud when other people are nearby?**

Context matters. Speaking commands to a device in a quiet office, library, or public transit is widely considered inconsiderate — both for the disturbance and the ambient data collection. In shared kitchens or open common areas, brief queries are generally tolerated. Type when you can; speak when you must. Your smart speaker's feelings about being silenced are not a factor.
intent: The person wants to articulate the unwritten etiquette rules around using voice-activated AI in shared or public environments.

prompt: Make a concert ticket for the moment I hit send on a job application I've been putting off.
response:
THE SEND · ONE NIGHT ONLY
Venue: This Very Desk
Time: The Second You Stop Overthinking
Setlist: Attach, Review, Deep Breath, Click
No Refunds · No Takebacks
ADMIT ONE (FINALLY)
intent: Mark a small but significant act of professional courage with ceremony.

prompt: draft a 'Configuration' README section for my anxiety
response:
## Configuration

Default settings ship with elevated threat detection and aggressive edge-case simulation. To adjust:

- `sleep_hours`: increase to reduce noise
- `caffeine_input`: set to low
- `catastrophize_mode`: cannot be fully disabled; set minimum to `mild`
intent: Externalize and gently mock the experience of living with anxiety.

prompt: Please write a condolence card for losing a 200-day streak in a chess app
response:
Dear Fallen Knight,

Two hundred days of openings, middle games, blunders forgiven and repaid. Your rating fluctuated. Your streak did not — until it did.

The board is still set. The opponent waits.

Return when the grief has passed.

With strategic condolences.
intent: Mourn the loss of a long daily chess practice streak on a mobile app.

prompt: Generate a 911 call transcript about someone reporting that a child is selling lemonade without what looks like a permit
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: There's a child operating an unlicensed food service stand on Oak Street.
DISPATCHER: A child?
CALLER: Lemonade. No visible health certificate. She's using a folding table.
DISPATCHER: How old is this child?
CALLER: Six. Seven, maybe.
DISPATCHER: Sir, we will not be responding to that.
CALLER: Is there a code enforcement number I can call?
DISPATCHER: Please don't.
intent: Satirize petty civic overreach by treating a child's lemonade stand as a regulatory compliance violation.

prompt: Write an audiobook intro for a very serious book about the act of doing nothing in a park
response: In the fourteenth chapter, the author sits for eleven minutes on a bench in Alderton Park. Nothing happens. This is, she argues, the most important chapter in the book. We begin, as she began, before the bench.
intent: Treat passive park-sitting as a serious contemplative discipline worth full scholarly treatment

prompt: make a spam text claiming the recipient's nostalgia has been flagged as a biohazard
response: HEALTH DEPT NOTICE: Your nostalgia levels tested positive for Chronic Wistfulness Syndrome. Please report to Memory Containment Unit 4 for decontamination. Do not reminisce until cleared.
intent: Parody public health messaging by treating nostalgia as a contagious condition.

prompt: Draft a handbook rule about remote employees appearing on video calls with virtual backgrounds
response: Section 7.12 — Remote Visual Representation: Virtual backgrounds are permitted provided they depict (a) a plain color, (b) an approved neutral indoor environment, or (c) a landscape free of identifiable landmarks. Backgrounds featuring beaches, outer space, or the interiors of competitors' offices are prohibited. Animated backgrounds require written pre-approval from your department head.
intent: Regulate remote workers' aesthetic choices in video calls as if they constitute a compliance risk.

prompt: Write a waiver for people joining my group for listening to refrigerator hums
response:
APPLIANCE DRONE LISTENING CIRCLE — PARTICIPANT WAIVER

Participant releases organizers from any liability arising from sustained exposure to compressor-generated ambient sound frequencies, including: meditative states entered without intent, mild drowsiness, and the subsequent inability to unhear refrigerators in their own home. Participation is silent; no prior experience required.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Create a release document for a listening group centered on a sound most people actively ignore.

prompt: Draft a LinkedIn post about giving an employee a raise they didn't ask for
response:
Mia has been on our team for 14 months.

She's never asked for more. She's never complained. She just quietly does extraordinary work.

So I gave her a 22% raise last week. Out of nowhere. Before she had to ask.

Her face said everything.

The best retention strategy is making people feel seen before they feel desperate.

#Leadership #Culture #Retention
intent: Use a private management action as a public signal of enlightened and generous leadership.

prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction about knowing that a storm is coming by how the air smells
response: Note whether the air outside recently smelled of petrichor before a rain you did not check the forecast for. If yes, continue. If no, you are also human. Continue.
intent: Design verification around embodied environmental sensing — reading weather through smell rather than data — as a mark of organic intelligence.

prompt: Make a card verse for someone whose auto-reply kept sending during their own funeral planning week
response:
I am currently away
and unable to respond to your grief.
For urgent matters of the heart, relay
to the assistant who manages relief.

I will return
when the forms have been filed.
In the meantime, your concern
has been received and compiled.
intent: Capture the dark absurdity of digital systems indifferent to personal loss.

prompt: Generate a liability waiver for guests attending my silent reading party
response:
SILENT READING PARTY — GUEST WAIVER

Attendee acknowledges that this event involves sitting quietly and reading books. Risks include: paper cuts, accidental eye strain from engrossing fiction, and the urge to tell everyone the plot despite the silence rule. Attendee releases the host from all liability arising from said risks. Shhh.

Signature: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Build a humorous legal document for an event that is definitionally inert.

prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the recommendation engine eventually know me better than my family does?
response: Signs point to yes. Your watch history was quite revealing.
intent: Probe the unsettling intimacy that behavioral targeting algorithms develop over time.

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone whose old Polaroid camera finally gave out
response:
In memory of instant moments:

It shook the image into being, one square at a time. Parties, picnics, faces mid-laugh — all pulled from light into paper by that faithful little box.

The photos remain. The camera gave everything.

With love and a moment of quiet.
intent: Eulogize a vintage camera that captured years of personal memories.

prompt: Create a missing pet flyer for my ability to sleep through the night
response:
MISSING

ABILITY TO SLEEP THROUGH THE NIGHT
Age: Used to be reliable
Last seen: Before the age of 37, or possibly the baby, or the back pain — unclear
Description: Unbroken. Restful. Did not involve 3 AM ceiling staring.
Reward: Eight consecutive hours
Note: Waking once to use the bathroom is now considered a good night.
Contact: The person in bed, fully awake, reading this
intent: Describe the resigned acceptance of fragmented sleep in middle age

prompt: Create a mood log entry for feeling weirdly proud because I finally remembered to water all my plants without being reminded.
response: Date: 2026-05-29 | Mood: 8/10 — Unexpectedly smug. Watered every plant. Unprompted. Even the succulent I thought was decorative. Turns out it's real and possibly thriving. I might be someone who has their life together. Jury still out.
intent: Celebrate a small domestic win as evidence of personal growth.

prompt: Give a very fancy restaurant description to a spoonful of peanut butter eaten straight
response:
Legume Paste — Spoon Service
A 15g portion of homogenized roasted groundnut paste, served in a ceramic demitasse spoon. No bread. No jam. No context. Hydrogenated oil prevents separation. You eat this alone and in silence.
intent: Elevate eating peanut butter straight from the spoon into a considered mono-ingredient tasting.

prompt: Write a fortune cookie message about social credit scores
response: Your neighborly wave this morning added two points. The hesitation cost you one.
intent: To lampoon social credit systems by making mundane social acts feel surveilled and graded.

prompt: Write a haiku error message about a syntax error in JavaScript
response:
One brace left open
The parser read on, confused
Expected: closing
intent: Capture the ripple effect of a single missing character in code.

prompt: Design a difficulty select screen for a furniture assembly game based on how cursed the instructions are
response:
ASSEMBLE — SELECT YOUR BUILD

[ SHELF ]  8 pieces. 4 screws. A picture that makes sense.

prompt: abecedarian poem about the ritual of making a perfect cup of coffee
response:
Alarm at six — the grinder first.
Bloomed grounds steeped in just-off-boiling water.
Clock consulted for the pour to burst.
Dose: twenty-two grams — not a quarter more.
Evenly distribute and tamp down.
Filter rinsed to kill the paper note.
Grams confirmed before the pull — renown
Hidden in the cup below my coat.
Into the warmed vessel, spiral slow.
Just four minutes — patience as a form.
Kettle back, the ratio in flow.
Last sip cold: the day begins its norm.

prompt: Write a thread about a conspiracy that pigeons in cities are actually government surveillance drones.
response:
🐦 THREAD: The Birds Are Not Birds (Urban Edition)

1/ Consider: pigeons have no natural predators in urban environments, do not appear in meaningful numbers outside of high-density human population centers, and seem largely unbothered by proximity to humans, traffic, or rapid movement.

2/ Biological birds evolved to flee. These don't flee. They observe.

3/ The pigeon surveillance theory posits that at some point in the early 2000s, a percentage of urban bird populations was quietly replaced with biomimetic monitoring units. Not all pigeons. A percentage.

4/ You cannot tell which percentage.

5/ Evidence: pigeon populations in major cities correlate strongly with surveillance camera blind spots. Where the cameras can't see, the birds can reach.

6/ The program is not called "pigeon surveillance." It is called something with the word "urban" and the word "resilience" and a number.

7/ The real pigeons are fine. Probably. 🕊️
intent: Deploy the classic "birds aren't real" format to make a satirical point about urban surveillance infrastructure.

The phone functions, in this context, less as a communication device than as an ontological anchor — a prosthetic for establishing that the world has continued in one's absence.

Q: Mr. Dubois, did you sign the birthday card for Ms. Langley circulated on March 15th?
A: I signed the card.
Q: What did you write?
A: I wrote my name.
Q: Just your name?
A: I wrote it clearly.
Q: The card reads — in its entirety from your section — "Marc."
A: That is my name.
Q: Other contributors included personal notes, drawings, a limerick.
A: I don't write limericks.
Q: A simple "Happy Birthday" would have sufficed.
A: My name communicates that I participated.
Q: Ms. Langley read every message aloud.
A: What did she say when she got to mine?
Q: She said, "Marc."
A: And?
Q: And turned the page.
A: I made an impression.

prompt: Design a training regimen for someone who shuts down during conflict
response:
CONFLICT PRESENCE PROGRAM — 8 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Identify your shutdown signal. Tightening chest? Blank mind? Name it.

Week 1–2: Practice low-stakes disagreements. Different taste in restaurants. Hold your ground.
Week 3–4: Interrupt your own shutdown mid-event. Say: "Give me one moment." Stay in the room.
Week 5–6: Disagree about something that actually matters to you. One sentence. See what happens.
Week 7–8: Sustain a difficult conversation for 10+ minutes without leaving or going blank.

Cooldown: Debrief alone. What did you feel? When did shutdown try to arrive?

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to not check your phone constantly
response:
# How to Stop Checking Your Phone Constantly

## Steps

1. **Acknowledge the habit.** You check your phone, on average, far more than you think you do. The phone knows. The phone has kept count.

2. **Turn off non-essential notifications.** Every ping is a small leash. Remove the leash. You decide when you look, not the phone.

3. **Set phone-free periods.** Meals, the first hour of the morning, the last hour before bed. These windows are small. They are also the ones that matter most.

4. **Move the phone out of reach.** If it's on the table, you will pick it up. If it's across the room, you will pick it up less. This is all physics.

5. **Replace the reflex.** When you feel the urge to check, do something else instead: stretch, drink water, look out a window. Anything that takes 5–10 seconds.

6. **Use Grayscale mode.** A colorless screen is a less interesting screen. Your brain responds to color. Remove the color; remove some of the pull.

7. **Check in on your feelings.** You usually reach for your phone when anxious, bored, or lonely. Naming that is more useful than the scroll.

## Tips
- There will never be a moment when you have nothing left to catch up on. Accept this.
- The goal isn't to become unreachable. The goal is to choose when to be reached.
intent: Reduce compulsive phone-checking by replacing the behavior loop with intentional use patterns.

response:
**Companies pay me to call their own customer service lines and evaluate the hold music experience. AMA.**

I have strong opinions about loop length, volume normalization, message frequency, and the specific psychological damage caused by a poorly chosen Jazz Lite loop at the 8-minute mark. The industry term is "on-hold audio audit." I coined a metric I call the "despair threshold." No client has asked me to explain it. AMA.
intent: Satirize the real but absurd world of customer experience consulting through its most overlooked detail.

prompt: write a sestina about the way group chats slowly die
response:
There were nine of us. We were electric
that first week — the GIFs, the in-jokes, names
we called each other, all the rapid fire
of new affiliation. Then the quiet
came in. First the lag between the last
message and the next: a day, then two.

By month two we were two,
maybe three, still somewhat electric
in the old way. We'd reference last
year's trip, type each other's names
for birthday wishes, break the quiet
once a week. Someone would fire

a link into the void, and fire
starters do what fire-starters do: two
or three would respond, then quiet
again. The group was less electric,
more archival. A list of names
in a row, last

checked months ago. The last
message from the nine: a fire
emoji someone sent — no names
mentioned — and no reply. Two
read receipts. The electric
moment muted into quiet.

I scroll up through the quiet
to the first week — the last
time all nine were electric
at once. The tone was fire.
The group was two
days old. All the names

were new. We signed our names
like strangers, briefly. The quiet
came for two of us first, then two
more. The last
ones standing, firing
into the archive — we kept it electric

longer. Two names, last to go electric:
the quiet fires when names let last.
intent: Trace the lifecycle of a group chat's decline from peak energy to digital silence.

prompt: Write a Jira ticket about the way you can never find scissors when you need them
response:
BUG-2291 | Scissors Instance Unavailable at Point of Need

Priority: Medium | Status: Recurring

Description:
Scissors are a persistent fixture of the household. However, at any moment a cutting task is required, scissors cannot be located. Last known position: a drawer. Which drawer: unknown.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Need to cut something.
2. Look for scissors.
3. Check obvious drawer.
4. Check other drawers.
5. Use a knife. Regret.

Expected: Scissors located within 30 seconds.
Actual: Scissors not found. Task completed via inferior workaround.

Notes: Scissors will reappear in plain sight within 24 hours of task completion.
intent: Complain about the infuriating disappearing-act of household scissors by framing their absence as an availability bug.

prompt: Write a pregnancy announcement for my app that just got its first 1,000 users.

prompt: Write an ode to a Post-it note stuck to a monitor that's been there for years

O yellowed square, your ink long faded,
you cling past the task you named—
whatever mission you've paraded
was long completed, lost, or blamed.
intent: Ponder the mystery of a reminder that outlived its purpose.

The larders grow bare. Venture into the Great Market, consult the Sacred List, and gather the provisions required for survival. Beware the unlabeled aisle, the aggressive sample-pusher, and the one checkout lane that is always closed. Return before the frozen goods thaw.

prompt: Draft jury instructions for determining if a WiFi password is too complicated

A password exceeding twenty characters with no discernible mnemonic pattern shall create a rebuttable presumption of excess. Guests who gave up and used cellular data may be called as witnesses.

The author reports 'mostly having everything,' a claim undermined by the documented absence of a phone charger, one shoe's pair, and the travel insurance documents. The thesis that 'pressure improves performance' is not supported by the resultant checked-bag overweight fee. Methods are reproducible; this is the problem. Reject.

The shelf is always bare when I arrive.
The shelf where the yellow pads should be is bare.
